EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer southeast wind increased the moisture over northern Arizona during the day on July 12th. Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Western Mogollon Rim near the town of Williams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 1.17 and 1.70 inches of rain in Williams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced strong outflow winds four miles north of Prescott.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a wind gust of 69 MPH four miles north of Prescott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the Gladiator Fire scar area caused heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just over an inch of rain fell in 35 minutes. A mud slide was reported on Gladiator Road between mile post 26 and 27. The road was still passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and non-life threatening flooding in and around Prescott.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Prescott Police Department reported many major stream and creek road crossings were closed due to flooding. One house in Williamson Valley started to incur non-life threatening flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm washed out a main road in Crown King.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and flash flooding over the Gladiator Fire area. A main road was washed out and there were also mud slides.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm washed out a main road in Crown King.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two vehicles were washed off the Crown King Road (possibly Poland Creek) near the community of Cleator. The occupants were rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rain and flash flooding near Cordes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported heavy flows though Dripping Springs and Turkey Creek. It was the worst flooding in eight and a half years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm with radar detected rotation produced golf ball sized hail in Cameron.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized (1.75 inches in diameter) fell at the Cameron Trading Post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain, strong winds, and lightning in Skull Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain filled several washes and closed several road crossings for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain, strong winds, and lightning in Skull Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind from a thunderstorm broke a branch out of a tree and it fell on power wires and knocked out power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced heavy rain, strong winds, and lightning in Skull Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a power pole and the pole caught fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rain in Payson and minor flooding issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 and 1.32 inches of rain fell in an hour in and around Payson. Flooding was reported everywhere. The worst was Forest Drive with two feet of water on the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and street flooding in Dewey-Humbolt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (2.10 inches) fell in Dewey-Humbolt. The intersection of Agua Fria and Prescott Street was close for a while due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rain (0.90 inches in 40 minutes), street flooding, and debris on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteriver Police reported 0.90 inches of rain in 40 minutes and debris on Route 73.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rain and street flooding in Tuba City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding in Tuba City with water entering homes. Highway 89 had water and debris on the roadway. Water was flowing in Moenkpi Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused debris to flow across Interstate 17 south of Sunset Rest Area. The road was closed after the debris punctured tires on several vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm washed debris across Interstate 17. The water and debris were about four inches deep. The road was closed after several vehicles punctured tires on the debris. The road opened after the flow stopped and the roadway was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen from the community of Rough Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was seen form the community of Rough Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain in the Paria River Basin in Utah. The water flowed southeast into Arizona and caused flash flooding in the Paria River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A river gage in Paria Canyon measured a four foot rise in less than 30 minutes on the Paria River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding and closed Highway 180 north of Alpine until debris could be cleared from the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell for 30 minutes northwest of Alpine. Three feet of water covered a country road. Highway 180 was also closed until the water receded and the debris was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding, mud, and rock slides over the Young Heber Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Fire area caused a mud and rock slide over Road 512 (Heber-Young Road). Road crews were called to clear the mud and debris from the road.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep and cold winter storm system appoaching Arizona brought strong winds and blowing dust to areas northeast of major terrain features such as the Mogollon Rim and San Fransisco Peaks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winslow ASOS reported less than 3 miles visibility in blowing dust for over two hours. A trained spotter one mile east of Winslow reported one mile visibility in blowing dust at 1255 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped heavy rain over the area that was burned by the Schultz Fire (2010) causing water to flow into the neighborhood below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz Fire area washed out a couple of driveways and a portion of Brandis Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure produced widespread valley rain and mountain snowfall. Valley rainfall amounts were generally one half of an inch to one inch. Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 17 inches in the mountains to the north and east of Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 17 inches of snowfall occurred at the top of Mt. Graham with this winter storm.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure produced widespread valley rain and mountain snowfall. Valley rainfall amounts were generally one half of an inch to one inch. Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 17 inches in the mountains to the north and east of Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 16 inches of snowfall occurred at Hannagan Meadow in the White Mountains of Greenlee County at an elevation around 9000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing Pacific low pressure system led to locally heavy snowfall across portions of southern Gila county beginning around midnight and continuing through the late morning hours on Monday, December 31st. The system was relatively cold, as snow levels fell to below 4000 feet on Monday morning; by 4 am snow was accumulating in the Globe/Miami area. Several inches of snow fell south of Globe, leading to slick and snowpacked roads which caused hazardous winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet and cold winter weather system moved slowly east across Arizona Sunday night into the morning hours on Monday, December 31st and it brought locally heavy snow to the lower elevations of southern Gila county. Snow levels fell  to around 3000 feet by mid morning on Monday, allowing snowfall to accumulate in the areas around Globe and Miami. According to a trained weather spotter, between 3 and 4 inches of new snow were measured 11 miles south of Cutter. The snowfall started shorly after midnight and continued until around noon. The snow led to slick and snow covered roads which created dangerous winter driving conditions. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued for this event on Sunday and then cancelled at 930 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front move from north to south across northern Arizona.  This brought high winds to the Peaks just north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at 11,526 feet above sea level on Agassiz Peak reported wind gusts over 60 MPH for over 10 hours. The peak wind gust was 88 MPH.  Arizona Snowbowl reported two snags (large dead trees) blew down near on or the lodges.  Other trees blew down on the 777 acre ski area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moving southeast over Arizona from Nevada produced a narrow band of heavy snow over the White Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The west side of Show Low received eight inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak short wave moved across Arizona while a stronger short wave moved across the Great Basin. Strong southwest winds produced substantial orographic uplift along the southwest facing slopes of higher terrain features of northern Arizona. The result was moderate to locally heavy snow across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sunrise Ski Park reported 13 inches of snow and Alpine had 6 to 8 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust, sand, and snow caused white/brown out conditions along Interstate 40 near Winslow. The visibility dropped to near zero at times. State police escorted travelers through this section of Interstate.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust, sand, and snow caused white/brown out conditions along Interstate 40 near Winslow. The visibility dropped to near zero at times. State police escorted travelers through this section of Interstate.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind sensor in Concho recorded sustained winds above 40 MPH and/or wind gusts above 58 MPH for close to three hours.  The peak wind gust was 62 MPH.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS in Winslow recorded sustained winds above 40 MPH and/or wind gusts above 58 MPH for a little over an hour.  The peak wind gust was 59 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm impacted Southeast Arizona on March 18th and March 19th producing snow in the mountains and in some of the lower elevations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 6 inches of snow occurred on Kitt Peak on the Baboquivari Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure over the Great Basin swept a strong cold front across Arizona.  Strong to very strong winds developed ahead of the front and produced areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust was reported in Many Farms.  The visibility was about 120 yards and below 50 yards at times.  The Police Department in Chinle reported poor visibility in blowing dust all day.  At least one employee had to stop on the road while driving to work because of the blowing dust.  The wind was estimated to be 40 to 50 MPH with gusts to 60 MPH.  Employees at a hotel in Kayenta reported 1/2 mile visibility in dust with estimated gusts to 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure over the Great Basin swept a strong cold front across Arizona.  Strong to very strong winds developed ahead of the front and produced areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The House Rock RAWS reported wind gusts of 58 MPH or higher for several hours during the afternoon.  The peak wind gust was 69 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in the community of Parks reported a peak wind gust of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mogollon Rim Airpark reported a peak wind gust of 62 MPH around 255 PM and Linden RAWS reported a peak wind gust of 61 MPH around 540 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen inches of snow fell in Forest Lakes. Seven inches of snow fell 3 miles southeast of Linden and 8.5 inches of snow fell in Heber-Overgaard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving across northern Arizona along with efficient up slope winds brought heavy snow to the North Rim of the Grand Canyon and Kaibab Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter at the North Rim of the Grand Canyon (over 8200 feet elevation) reported 11.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong northwest winds behind a cold front hit the Kachina Peaks just north of Flagstaff on Christmas day.  The sensor was damaged in the wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind sensor at 11,526 feet on Agassiz Peak reported wind gusts over 80 MPH for 30 minutes. The winds gusted over 60 MPH for close to 6 hours.  The wind sensor was damaged by the wind. Trees were knocked down near that elevations.  A local ski resort employee said that the wind likely gusted over 100 MPH before the sensor was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system brought snow to much of northern Arizona with measurable snow down to 3400 feet elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over eight inches of snow fell just north and northeast of Payson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system move from off the southern California coast, across southern Nevada, and then northern Arizona. This brought accumulating snow down to as low at 3500 feet.  The heaviest snow fell over the Eastern Mogollon Rim and White Mountains.  Winter Storm conditions were seen at lower elevations (such as Page) that do not usually see much snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Show Low received 6 to 7 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm impacted Southeast Arizona on March 18th and March 19th producing snow in the mountains and in some of the lower elevations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 1 foot of snow occurred at Hannagan Meadow in Northern Greenlee County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm impacted Southeast Arizona on March 18th and March 19th producing snow in the mountains and in some of the lower elevations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 3 to 4 inches of snow occurred at Oracle in Southeast Pinal County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm impacted Southeast Arizona on March 18th and March 19th producing snow in the mountains and in some of the lower elevations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 40 MPH with gusts to 55 MPH occurred at Douglas-Bisbee airport.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm impacted Southeast Arizona on March 18th and March 19th producing snow in the mountains and in some of the lower elevations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow occurred in elevations from about 3500 to 4000 feet in Graham County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sedona received 4 inches of snow at an elevation of about 4500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind sensor in Fraziers Well recorded sustained winds above 40 MPH and/or wind gusts above 58 MPH for close to an hour.  The peak wind gust was 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind sensor in Linden recorded sustained winds above 40 MPH and/or wind gusts above 58 MPH for close to three hours.  The peak wind gust was 69 MPH. THe Mogollon Airpark reported a peak wind gust of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two different wind sensors in Doney Park (northeast of Flagstaff on the lee side of the San Francisco Peaks) reported peak wind gusts of 61 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system move from off the southern California coast, across southern Nevada, and then northern Arizona. This brought accumulating snow down to as low at 3500 feet.  The heaviest snow fell over the Eastern Mogollon Rim and White Mountains.  Winter Storm conditions were seen at lower elevations (such as Page) that do not usually see much snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of snow fell in Page at an elevation of 4200 feet. This much snow at this elevation meets the heavy snow criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system move from off the southern California coast, across southern Nevada, and then northern Arizona. This brought accumulating snow down to as low at 3500 feet.  The heaviest snow fell over the Eastern Mogollon Rim and White Mountains.  Winter Storm conditions were seen at lower elevations (such as Page) that do not usually see much snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter at Show Low Lake reported 8.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought strong and gusty winds to northern Arizona and locally heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front brought high wind to the Eastern Mogollon Rim during the early morning hours.  The peak wind gust in Linden was 61 MPH.  The Limestone RAWS recorded peak wind gusts between 57 and 59 MPH between 100 AM and 5 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds in Kingman knocked down trees and power lines and damaged two carports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Isolated heavy rain and snow also fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Pipe Spring National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.  Isolated heavy rain and snow also fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at the Kingman Airport (KIGM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred 8 miles SW of Littlefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Payson and Pine both received 7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Prescott area received 5 to 14 inches of snow depending on elevation. The City of Prescott is at about 5280 feet and the snow level started out above that level and then dropped through the night.  Ash Fork received 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the deserts of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, during the afternoon hours on July 29th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall with rainfall rates in excess of 1.5 inches per hour. The excessive rain led to episodes of flash flooding, primarily in the southeast portions of the Phoenix area, and unfortunately led to the death of a 2 year old boy who was swept down a flooded wash in Queen Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southwestern Maricopa county during the evening hours on July 29th, and produced locally heavy rain in the area around Gila Bend. Radar indicated that rainfall rates were well in excess of 1 inch per hour, and the heavy rain prompted the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory for the Gila Bend area at 807 pm. According to the Gila Bend Fire Department, at 1020 pm, a swift water rescue was needed when 5 children became stuck in the middle of a flooded wash at 307th Avenue and Watermelon Road. The report was obtained via  a Twitter feed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in Douglas, causing it to catch fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a two-story house at 11th Street and C Avenue in Douglas causing the house to catch fire. Firefighters extinguished the fire in 20 minutes.  Smoke damage was confined to the second story.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seasonably hot temperatures caused a heat related fatality in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures of 100 to 106 degrees resulted in the death of a construction worker from Indiana.  Paramedics were called to treat a man suffering from cardiac arrest who had been suffering from heat-related illness for two hours before his collapse. He was pronounced dead at 455 pm at the construction site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry weather caused the drought to continue across portions of southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued in portions of Pinal County throughout June. The overall D3 area remained unchanged from May. While some areas actually received more rain than normal, those spotty rains amounts only totaled between a tenth and a half of an inch and therefore did little to improve drought conditions, especially since temperatures were above normal.  In fact, the Tucson airport experienced its 5th warmest June on record and had 28 days of 100 degrees or warmer. Since the impacts of this drought began with the winter of 2010-2011, most have already been mitigated. The extent of the dry weather was manifested in dust storm events that occurred on several dates in June.  Thunderstorms that brought the little rain that did fall, also created gusty outflow winds that kicked up dust, ultimately developing into haboobs.  These large areas of blowing dust rolled northward across portions of Pima and Pinal Counties toward Phoenix on three occasions in June.  Two of these dust storms reduced visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile on Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the early afternoon hours and they generated gusty ouflow winds which spread into the south central deserts, including the community of Casa Grande. The gusty winds picked up significant dust and generated dust storm conditions that persisted through mid afternoon. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the dust storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona on July 22nd and they produced gusty outflow winds up to 40 mph. The gusty winds spread to the northwest and into the central deserts and created dust storm conditions. At 235 pm, the Department of Public Safety reported visibility down to 30 feet in dense blowing dust on Interstate 10 at mile marker 205. The location was just southwest of Toltec. Additional reports from the area indicated that tree limbs had been downed due to the strong winds. In addition, a Ham radio report indicated a dust storm at the Casa Grande Airport where winds of 40 mph lowered visibility below one quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the south central Arizona deserts during the afternoon hours on the 14th, and they continued into the evening. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding or flooding. Radar and rain gages indicated that rainfall rates exceeded 3 inches per hour at times. A few of the storms produced wet microbursts with damaging wind in excess of 60 mph. Much of the heavy rain and flash flooding affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern portions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern valley communities, during the afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated intense rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding. Heavy rain in excess of 2 inches fell in the Gilbert area between 330 pm and 430 pm. During the peak of the heavy rainfall, approximately 1.5 inches of rain fell within 10 minutes, causing the collapse of the roof at Tees and More located at 313 North Gilbert Road. According to the store owners, Pete and Barb Addiego, the roof collapse caused at least 110 thousand dollars in damaged equipment and inventory. Fortunately, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved across portions of southern Gila county during the afternoon hours on July 20th. The storms produced heavy rain; rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour were observed and the heavy rain led to the issuance of small stream flood advisories for the Globe/Miami area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southern Gila county during the afternoon hours on July 20th, and the storms produced locally heavy rainfall with rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour. At 419 pm, a trained weather spotter one mile east of Globe reported 0.87 inches of rainfall with the past 40 minutes. At 349 pm, another weather spotter in Globe reported one half of an inch of rain within 20 minutes, a rainfall rate of 1.5 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory for southern Gila county, including the Globe area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific low pressure system over the desert southwest led to gusty winds in excess of 40 mph across the central Arizona deserts during the afternoon hours on February 27th. The strong winds generated areas of blowing dust, lowering visibilities to one quarter mile or less and leading to the issuance of a dust storm warning. The dust storm warning affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful Pacific low pressure system over the desert southwest led to strong, gusty winds in excess of 40 mph across the central Arizona deserts during the afternoon hours on February 27th. Shortly after noon, a dust storm warning was issued for the greater Phoenix area, and at 1250 pm a trained weather spotter 2 miles southwest of Firebird lake reported a dust storm. The spotter indicated that visibility had dropped below one quarter mile at State Route 347 and Queen Creek Road. The dust storm warning was cancelled at approximately 3 pm. No accidents or injuries were reported with this dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. They generated strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which moved into the greater Phoenix area during the late afternoon and produced dense blowing dust. Some of the storms then moved into the southeast portions of the greater Phoenix area and produced strong, damaging microburst winds which enhanced the earlier outflow winds. A trained weather spotter in far East Mesa reported a variety of wind damage at approximately 545 pm. He reported damage to an awning, a carport taken off by wind, windows blown out and cactus trees blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved into the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the evening hours on July 21st. The storms were accompanied by strong gusty outflow winds, and dense blowing dust. Some of the storms generated damaging microburst winds as well. At 710 pm, there was a public report of 2 trees downed by wind about 6 miles east of Peoria. The wind gusts were estimated to be about 60 knots, and the trees were rather large with diameters of 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain of southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms produced strong outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which moved to the northwest and into the south central deserts during the late afternoon hours. The strong winds picked up substantial dust and dirt and created a massive dust storm which moved into the greater Phoenix metropolitan area after about 5 pm. Numerous weather spotters reported winds above 40 mph and visibilities below one quarter of a mile in a dust storm. The near zero visibilities created very hazardous driving conditions and led to some traffic accidents. No serious injuries or fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms generated strong outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which spread to the northwest and picked up massive quantities of dust. A dust storm was created which moved into the the southeast portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area after 430 pm...and continued to overspread the area through 630 pm. Numerous trained weather spotters reported dust storm conditions with winds gusting in excess of 40 mph, and visibilities below one quarter mile in dense blowing dust. A trained spotter 4 miles west of Apache Junction reported a dust storm at 447 pm, with 40 mph winds and visibility below one eighth of a mile. At 527 pm a storm chaser near Firebird Lake reported a dust storm with periods of visibility down to 100 feet. Another trained spotter near Firebird Lake reported that emergency vehicles had responded to an accident caused by the blowing dust. Finally, at about 5 pm a trained spotter near Seville reported a wind gust to 52 mph along with zero visibility in dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved eastward across southern Arizona on May 9th. Southeast flow ahead of the low advected moisture into eastern Arizona, and scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving east across southern Arizona spread moisture into eastern Arizona and led to scattered thunderstorms across the central deserts during the afternoon hours on May 9th. A few storms generated gusty and damaging winds estimated to be in excess of 60 knots. At 439 PM, a trained weather spotter reported that small trees and large limbs were down over the road at Ray and Cooper in Gilbert. Another weather spotter in Chandler reported that three large trees, all of them 36 inches wide, were blown over near south Dobson road and East Market Place at approximately 5 PM. Pictures of the downed trees were taken and sent to the Phoenix WCM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low presssure system moving east across southern Arizona on May 9th spread moisture into eastern Arizona, and this led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over the lower central deserts during the afternoon hours. Some of the storms produced gusty microburst winds in excess of 60 knots, and the outflow winds moving over dry ground produced a series of dust storms which affected both the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and the area around Gila Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving east across southern Arizona spread moisture into eastern Arizona and led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over the central deserts during the afternoon hours on May 9th. The storms generated gusty microburst winds in excess of 60 knots, and the outflow winds moving over dry ground produced a series of dust storms. Beginning at around 4 PM, several trained spotters in the Coolidge and Casa Grande areas reported visibilities at or below one quarter mile in dense blowing dust. Spotters indicated that wind gusts were 35 to 40 mph or greater as the dust storm moved through the area. The dense blowing dust created very hazardous driving conditions during the afternoon rush hour, however no significant accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong low pressure system brought widespread high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds tore shingles off several buildings 2 miles E of Meadview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over portions of La Paz county during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 31st. Due to the excessively moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms that developed produced copious amounts of rain, with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rainfall led to episodes of flash flooding, which affected the communities of Quartzside and Ehrenberg. In addition, a few storms were able to generate damaging wet microburst winds, which were strong enough to down a number of power poles in the Parker area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across La Paz county during the late morning and early afternoon hours on July 31st. In addition to producing heavy rain, a few of the storms generated strong and damaging wet microburst winds, estimated to be at least 50 knots. Local law enforcement reported that power lines were down along Arizona highway 95 both north and south of Parker. The Parker airport experienced a peak wind gust to 56 mph with the storms, and according to APS, 7 power poles were downed in the Parker area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved steadily east across Arizona on February 14, and brought scattered rain showers to the central deserts along with locally heavy snowfall to the higher terrain areas east of Phoenix. Several inches of wet snow fell in the area around Globe during the late morning and afternoon hours, causing slick and hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved slowly east across Arizona on February 14th, and it brought scattered to numerous rain showers to the central deserts as well as locally heavy snow to the higher elevations east of Phoenix. At 210 pm, a trained weather spotter in Globe reported 3 inches of heavy wet snowfall just north of Globe. Area roads such as U.S. 60 and State Route 77 were reported to be slick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific low pressure system brought scattered rain and snow showers to southern Gila county beginning shortly after midnight on February 28th. During the early morning hours the snow level lowered to below 4000 feet creating slick and hazardous road conditions across southern Gila county, including the Globe and Miami areas. The slick roads led to car accidents, no injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific low pressure system brought rain and snow showers to southern Gila county, beginning shortly after midnight. Snow levels lowered to below 4000 feet by early morning and slick roads developed in the area around Globe and Miami. Heavy snow was not reported, but the amounts were sufficient to cause hazardous driving conditions. According to law enforcement personnel, at around 5 am a car accident occurred on Highway 77 south of Globe, due to the slick road conditions. Snow amounts at the time were less than one inch. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved through south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st, and some of them produced gusty and damaging microburst winds. According to a trained weather spotter 6 miles east of the Tonto National Monument, at 415 pm thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph uprooted a 20 inch diameter Mulberry tree. In addition, the strong winds tore metal off of a storage shed and ripped some shingles off of a nearby roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain of southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours, then moved towards the northwest and into the greater Phoenix area by late afternoon. In addition to generating dense blowing dust, the storms also created strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds. At 5 pm, a trained weather spotter 2 miles west of Apache Junction reported that strong winds had ripped a number of shingles off of his roof. He estimated the wind gusts to be at least 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved eastward across southern Arizona on May 9th. Southeast flow ahead of the low advected moisture into eastern Arizona, and scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southeast flow ahead of a vigorous upper level low pressure system spread moisture into south central Arizona on May 9th. This led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over the central deserts during the afternoon and early evening hours. A few storms in the greater Phoenix area produced damaging microburst winds, estimated to be 50 knots or higher. According to SRP, a local utility company, the winds downed 9 12-kilovolt poles, most of them in north-central Phoenix. Over 4800 people were without power at the peak of the storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over portions of La Paz county during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 31st. Due to the excessively moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms that developed produced copious amounts of rain, with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rainfall led to episodes of flash flooding, which affected the communities of Quartzside and Ehrenberg. In addition, a few storms were able to generate damaging wet microburst winds, which were strong enough to down a number of power poles in the Parker area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county, including the community of Ehrenberg, during the late morning and early afternoon hours on July 31st. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the airmass, the storms produced locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain prompted the issuance of a Flash Flood Warning for the Ehrenberg area at 1137 am. At 1115 am, the Parker Sheriff's Office reported flooded highways in Ehrenberg. At 1155 am, the La Paz County Sheriff's Office reported a flash flood across Mohave road, and the road had been closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry weather caused the drought to continue across portions of southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued in portions of Pinal County throughout June. The overall D3 area remained unchanged from May. While some areas actually received more rain than normal, those spotty rains amounts only totaled between a tenth and a half of an inch and therefore did little to improve drought conditions, especially since temperatures were above normal.  In fact, the Tucson airport experienced its 5th warmest June on record and had 28 days of 100 degrees or warmer. Since the impacts of this drought began with the winter of 2010-2011, most have already been mitigated. The extent of the dry weather was manifested in dust storm events that occurred on several dates in June.  Thunderstorms that brought the little rain that did fall, also created gusty outflow winds that kicked up dust, ultimately developing into haboobs.  These large areas of blowing dust rolled northward across portions of Pima and Pinal Counties toward Phoenix on three occasions in June.  Two of these dust storms reduced visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile on Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and rather unstable airmass was in place across southern Arizona on the 4th of July. A weather disturbance moving into southern Arizona from Mexico led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of south central Arizona during the morning hours. A cluster of thunderstorms over western Pinal county led to locally heavy rainfall in the area around Casa Grande; 3 hourly rainfall totals in excess of 1.5 inches were observed. This led to urban flooding, especially in the community of Arizona City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms containing locally heavy rainfall developed across south central Arizona during the morning hours on July 4th, and they affected the greater Phoenix area. A trained weather spotter in Surprise, near the intersection of Greenway and Reems roads, indicated that approximately 1.5 inches of rain fell between 1115 am and 1230 pm. Nearly three quarters of an inch fell in 45 minutes, and the heavy rain flooded their yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over portions of La Paz county during the late morning and afternoon hours on July 31st. Due to the excessively moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms that developed produced copious amounts of rain, with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rainfall led to episodes of flash flooding, which affected the communities of Quartzside and Ehrenberg. In addition, a few storms were able to generate damaging wet microburst winds, which were strong enough to down a number of power poles in the Parker area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county, including the areas around Quartzsite, during the late morning and early afternoon hours on July 31st. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the airmass, the storms produced locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, and at 1137 am a Flash Flood Warning was issued for areas around Quartzsite. According to local law enforcement, at 1240 pm washes were running in numerous locations north of Quartzsite, forcing the closure of Highway 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and rather unstable airmass was in place across southern Arizona on the 4th of July. A weather disturbance moving into southern Arizona from Mexico led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of south central Arizona during the morning hours. A cluster of thunderstorms over western Pinal county led to locally heavy rainfall in the area around Casa Grande; 3 hourly rainfall totals in excess of 1.5 inches were observed. This led to urban flooding, especially in the community of Arizona City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed in the area from Casa Grande southeast to Arizona City; the storms produced locally heavy rainfall during the late morning hours on July 4th. Radar estimates, as well as surrounding rain gage reports, suggested that three hourly totals between one and two inches occurred in the community of Arizona City between 9 am and noon. A weather report received via CoCoRaHS indicated 1.05 inches of rain fell in Arizona City, as of 9 am. Pinal county law enforcement reported road closures in Arizona City due to heavy rainfall. According to a local news report, a number of residents of Arizona City experienced significant urban flooding during the morning due to the heavy rains; water up to a foot deep flooded their subdivisions and flowed into their homes, causing water damage to the first floor as well as their patios/pavers. Insufficent drainage allowed the locally heavy rains to quickly pond up across the lower elevations of Arizona City, and the water levels remained high through the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed over the central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on July 10th. Some of the storms produced gusty and damaging outflow winds, which were strong enough to blow down a tree in the community of Glendale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the evening hours across the central Arizona deserts. Some of the storms produced gusty and damaging outflow winds. At 945 pm, a trained weather spotter 5 miles northeast of Glendale reported a tree blown down at 52nd Avenue and Thunderbird road. The wind gusts were estimated to be around 50 knots.

Some of the storms produced gusty microburst winds in excess of 60 knots, and the outflow winds moving over dry ground produced a series of dust storms which affected both the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and the area around Gila Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving east across southern Arizona spread moisture into eastern Arizona and led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over the central deserts during the afternoon hours on May 9th. The storms generated gusty microburst winds in excess of 60 knots, and the outflow winds moving over dry ground produced a series of dust storms; one of these affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. At 427 PM, a trained spotter four miles south of Gilbert reported a dust storm. The dense blowing dust primarily affected the southern and southeast portions of the greater Phoenix area. The dense blowing dust created hazardous driving conditions during the afternoon rush hour, fortunately no significant accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low presssure system moving east across southern Arizona on May 9th spread moisture into eastern Arizona, and this led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over the lower central deserts during the afternoon hours. Some of the storms produced gusty microburst winds in excess of 60 knots, and the outflow winds moving over dry ground produced a series of dust storms which affected both the greater Phoenix metropolitan area and the area around Gila Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving east across southern Arizona spread moisture into eastern Arizona and led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over the central deserts during the afternoon hours on May 9th. The storms generated gusty microburst winds in excess of 60 knots, and the outflow winds moving over dry ground produced a series of dust storms. One of the dust storms moved into southwest Maricopa county late in the afternoon and impacted the community of Gila Bend. At 616 PM, the official weather station at the Gila Bend airport measured a peak wind gust to 49 knots and reported zero mile visibility in a dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms that developed during the evening on July 11th continued to affect the central Arizona deserts during the early morning hours on July 12th. In addition to locally heavy rainfall, which prompted the issuance of areal flood advisories, a few storms managed to produce gusty and damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, which developed over the south central Arizona deserts during the evening hours on July 11th, persisted into the early morning hours on July 12th. Although most of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall, a few managed to generate gusty and damaging outflow winds. According to local broadcast media, shortly after midnight wind gusts estimated at 60 knots blew down a billboard at 24th Street and University. A few large trees were also uprooted. The damage occurred about 2 miles west of Phoenix Sky Harbor airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms produced strong and gusty outflow winds which moved northwest and into the Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon. The winds, which were in excess of 50 mph at times, generated dense blowing dust and dust storm conditions. At 511 pm, a trained weather spotter 2 miles southwest of Firebird Lake, or in far southwest Chandler, measured a wind gust to 63 mph. At the time, a dust storm was in progress with zero visibility in dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st, and they generated strong and gusty outflow winds which moved to the northwest and into the greater Phoenix area in the late afternoon. At 555 pm, the KIWA AWOS weather station at Phoenix Mesa-Gateway Airport recorded a measured wind gust to 52 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil that formed on a relatively warm and calm day damaged one structure in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil formed in a church parking lot near Pima and Swan in Tucson.  The dust devil grew and moved over the church, displacing and damaging a number of roof tiles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north winds behind a cold front accelerated down the Colorado River Valley, causing isolated minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds damaged a power line, knocking out power to over 2,000 customers in the Bullhead City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moist fetch of subtropical air fueled locally heavy rain which caused isolated damage over the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the roof of a building in Bullhead City to collapse.  The interior was damaged as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on June 27th. The storms produced gusty outflow winds which spread to the northwest and generated dense blowing dust across portions of the central deserts, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Dust storms were reported by a number of trained weather spotters, as visibilities dropped below one quarter of a mile. In addition to the dense blowing dust, damaging microburst winds were also reported in the community of Toltec, in northern Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on June 27th. The storms generated strong and gusty outflow winds which spread into portions of northern Pinal county. According to law enforcement personnel, at about 615 pm microburst wind gusts estimated to be near 70 knots overturned a double wide trailer in the community of Toltec. The strong winds also blew down a number of power poles in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the middle afternoon hours on June 16th, and they produced gusty outflow winds as high as 45 mph. The winds spread to the northwest, picking up substantial amounts of dust and dirt and generating a dust storm. The dust storm moved through northwest Pinal county, through communities such as Coolidge and Casa Grande, and then into the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across southeastern Arizona during the middle of the afternoon on June 16th, and they produced gusty outflow winds as high as 45 mph. The winds spread to the northwest and produced a dust storm; at approximately 5 pm a spotter in Casa Grande reported a haboob approaching from the southeast. The haboob then moved into the southeast portion of the greater Phoenix area during the late afternoon hours. At 520 pm a trained spotter at Gilbert and Riggs road reported visibility below 1/8 of a mile in dense blowing dust. The dust then moved into the west valley; at about 7 pm weather spotters in Suprise and north Glendale reported blowing dust with visibilities below 1/4 of a mile. The dense blowing dust produced hazardous driving conditions to motorists across the greater Phoenix area, fortunately no major accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on June 27th. The storms produced gusty outflow winds which spread to the northwest and generated dense blowing dust across portions of the central deserts, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Dust storms were reported by a number of trained weather spotters, as visibilities dropped below one quarter of a mile. In addition to the dense blowing dust, damaging microburst winds were also reported in the community of Toltec, in northern Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on June 27th. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 45 mph spread to the northwest and into the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours, producing dust storm conditions. At about 8 pm, a trained spotter in central Phoenix reported visibility down to 200 feet at US Airways Center. At 740 pm, another spotter in Ahwatukee reported a dust storm with visibility less than 1/4 mile at 42nd & Frye. In addition, the KIWA AWOS automatic weather station indicated 1/4 mile visibility and 46 mph wind gusts shortly after 7 pm. The dense blowing dust produced very hazardous driving conditions across the greater Phoenix area, especially during the latter portions of the rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona on the 13th, and some of them produced heavy rain over portions of southern Gila county. The heavy rain led to local flooding of streams and roads, affecting communities such as Globe and Cutter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southern Gila county during the morning hours on July 13th, and they persisted into the afternoon. The storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to running washes, flooding of area streets and overflowing creeks. At 1128 am, a trained spotter in Globe reported street flooding. At 1240 pm, law enforcement personnel indicated that a creek was overflowing approximately 3 miles southeast of Cutter. No reports of injuries or property damage were received in response to the heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across the deserts of southwest Maricopa county during the early afternoon hours on July 31st. The storms developed strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which moved towards the northeast and into the central deserts, including areas to the south and east of the greater Phoenix area. The winds picked up significant dust and caused dense blowing dust across northwest Pinal county, from Casa Grande northwestward towards the Pinal county line. The dense dust led to the issuance of both a Blowing Dust Advisory and a Dust Storm Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across southwest Maricopa county during the early afternoon hours on July 31st. The storms generated gusty outflow winds in excess of 35 mph which moved to the northeast and into the central deserts, including portions of northwest Pinal county. The gusty winds created dense blowing dust, and dust storm conditions, primarily in an area from Sun Lakes through Casa Grande. At 435 pm, a trained spotter 2 miles southeast of Sun Lakes reported visibility less than one quarter mile in dense blowing dust. Another spotter located along Interstate 10 at mile marker 174 near the Gila River reported visibility ranging from 20 feet to 500 yards in a dust storm. Finally, a spotter at the intersection of 587 and the Hunt Highway indicated that visibility was down to 100 feet at times in dense blowing dust. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense blowing dust, which created hazardous driving conditions during the evening rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable atmosphere was present across southern Arizona on July 14th, and scattered thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. Most of the stronger storms produced locally heavy rainfall, but isolated storms over southern Gila county were able to produce some large hail. No damage was reported as a result of the hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms, many with heavy rain, developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 14th. A few of the storms were able to produce moderate to large hail. At 330 pm, a trained weather spotter in Globe reported large hail, measured at three quarters of an inch in diameter. At approximately the same time,  another spotter 2 miles northwest of the Boyce Thompson Arboretum reported hail one quarter of an inch in diameter. No damage was reported due to the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the south central Arizona deserts during the afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms tended to produce locally heavy rainfall with rainfall rates well in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to some flash flooding in the Wickenburg area late in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 14th, and some of them affected the Wickenburg area. Due to the very moist and unstable  nature of the atmosphere, the storms tended to produce heavy rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding. A flash flood warning had been issued for the Wickenburg area starting at 503 pm on the 14th. According to a trained weather spotter, at 618 pm the heavy rains led to a mudslide which blocked the westbound lanes of US 60 at mile marker 115.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the south central Arizona deserts during the afternoon hours on the 14th, and they continued into the evening. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding or flooding. Radar and rain gages indicated that rainfall rates exceeded 3 inches per hour at times. A few of the storms produced wet microbursts with damaging wind in excess of 60 mph. Much of the heavy rain and flash flooding affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern portions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the very wet and unstable nature of the airmass, most of the thunderstorms produced very heavy rain. A few generated wet microbursts with damaging winds. At 345 pm, a Weather Service employee reported that numerous trees had been blown down at the San Tan Village Mall in Gilbert Ranch. One of the trees was about 40 feet tall and it fell over and landed on an automobile, causing significant damage. Peak wind gusts were estimated to be about 65 knots. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix area during the mid afternoon hours on July 31st. One storm produced a wind gust to 64 mph at the Deer Valley airport. The thunderstorms moved slowly north, then congealed and intensified over the community of Anthem. Very heavy rains, with rainfall rates in excess of 3 inches per hour, were observed and they led to episodes of flash flooding in the community of Anthem. One weather observer reported rainfall in excess of 5 inches in his rain gage. Flooding also occurred in the Deadman Wash near Interstate 17 to the west of Anthem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on July 31st. Some of the storms affected the community of Deer Valley and they generated strong microburst winds. At 445 pm, the official ASOS weather station at the Deer Valley airport recorded a peak wind gust of 56 knots, or 64 mph. No damage was reported at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A drier than average winter and spring contributed to a continued active fire season in late June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Canyon Fire was reported in Jacks Canyon about 30 miles southwest of Winslow just after 700 AM on June 29. The fire was contained at 6935 acres on July 5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across Arizona brought widespread strong winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 MPH was reported at the Mogollon Airpark in Heber-Overgaard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across Arizona brought widespread strong winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sheriff Office in Kayenta reported blowing dust with the visibility down to 1/2 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to the White Mountains of Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Show Low Airport recorded a peak wind gust of 58 MPH at 2:15 PM.  The sustained wind also exceeded 40 MPH several times between 12:55 PM and 3:40 PM.

Due to the very moist and unstable airmass in place, most of the storms generated locally heavy rain, with the highest rain rates observed to be in excess of 2 inches per hour. According to a trained weather spotter in Gilbert, 2.25 inches of rain fell between 330 pm and 430 pm at a home located at Val Vista and Elliot roads. The heavy rain led to some street flooding at the San Tan Village Mall. Marble sized hail also occurred, along with some gusty winds that damaged a few trees in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain of southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms produced strong outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which moved to the northwest and into the south central deserts during the late afternoon hours. The strong winds picked up substantial dust and dirt and created a massive dust storm which moved into the greater Phoenix metropolitan area after about 5 pm. Numerous weather spotters reported winds above 40 mph and visibilities below one quarter of a mile in a dust storm. The near zero visibilities created very hazardous driving conditions and led to some traffic accidents. No serious injuries or fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered  thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms produced strong outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which spread northwest across the deserts of northern Pinal county. The gusty winds picked up copious amounts of dust and dirt and generated dust storm conditions. At 4 pm a trained spotter near Arizola reported one eighth of a mile visibility in blowing dust; by 418 pm visibility dropped to zero mile and cars were pulled onto the side of the road. At 428 pm a spotter 7 miles east of Sacaton reported a dust storm with zero visibility and wind gusts to 35 mph. In addition, spotters in communities such as San Tan Valley and Apache Junction reported dust storm conditions with winds gusting as high as 50 mph.

During the latter portion of the afternoon, a few storms in the Anthem area congealed and intensified. At 630 pm, a trained weather spotter 2 miles north of Anthem reported  that gusty microburst winds had torn up a number of trees throughout his neighborhood. The peak wind gusts were estimated to be in excess of 60 knots. In addition, the storms produced half inch hail and very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms develooped across the higher terrain of southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms then moved towards the northwest and into south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the late afternoon and early evening. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds, in addition to locally heavy rainfall. Significant flash flooding did not occur, but urban flooding of streets and washes was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st and they spread northwestward and into the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours. In addition to strong gusty winds and dense blowing dust, some of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to urban flooding.  At 745 pm a trained spotter 6 miles east of Peoria reported heavy rain which caused local washes to run and curb to curb street flooding. In addition, a local retention basin had filled up due to the heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st. The storms produced gusty outflow winds which spread westward across the lower deserts during the evening hours. The winds picked up substantial amounts of dust and sand and generated dust storm conditions which spread into the southwest Arizona deserts after 7 pm. No injuries were reported associated with the dust storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southeast Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 21st and they generated strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph. The winds picked up substantial amounts of dust and sand as they spread west, and created dust storm conditions over the southwest Arizona deserts. At 730 pm a trained weather spotter in Dateland reported a dust storm, with visibility down to one eighth of a mile in dense blowing dust. A dust storm warning was issued shortly afterwards for the southwest Arizona deserts. No accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the south central Arizona deserts during the afternoon hours on the 14th, and they continued into the evening. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding or flooding. Radar and rain gages indicated that rainfall rates exceeded 3 inches per hour at times. A few of the storms produced wet microbursts with damaging wind in excess of 60 mph. Much of the heavy rain and flash flooding affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern portions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern valley communities, during the afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated locally heavy rainfall which led to widespread street flooding. According to a trained weather spotter 2 miles southwest of Gilbert, 1.1 inches of rain fell between 330 pm and 4 pm. Several additional weather spotters in the southeast valley, including communities such as Gilbert, Chandler and Seville, reported similar amounts of rainfall between 330 pm and 5 pm. Commonly, the reports indicated that around one inch of rain fell within a 30 minute period. For example, at 442 pm a trained spotter 2 miles west of Apache Junction reported 0.93 inches of rain measured in the past half-hour.

Much of the heavy rain and flash flooding affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern portions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeast valley communities, during the afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated locally heavy rain which led to flooding and flash flooding across portions of the southeast valley. Heavy rain began by 3 pm or so, and a flash flood warning was issued starting at 343 pm. According to Arizona Department of Transportation (ADOT) personnel, the excessive rain caused four lanes of US 60 - the Superstition Freeway - to be blocked off in each direction, approximately 2 miles south of Mesa. The freeway was blocked by 430 pm and remained closed well into the evening.

At about 4 pm, the upper Sycamore Creek Canyon precipitation gage on the upper portion of the burn reported 0.87 inches of rain within a 15 minute period. This amount of rain, coupled with additional rainfall, led to flash flooding along Sycamore Creek as well as the burn area and the community of Sunflower. At 551 pm, DPS reported that Sycamore wash was flooding with water reaching homes in Sunflower. At 556 pm, a report from ArizonaNewsnet indicated a flood approaching the Sycamore Creek Beeline Highway bridge at milepost 212. Additional reports near 6 pm indicated water was out of the banks of Sycamore creek and covering area roads in and around Sunflower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month of well below normal rainfall resulted in continued extreme drought conditions across portions of south central Arizona in May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued in portions Pinal County throughout May. The overall D3 area remained unchanged from April.  While portions of southeast Arizona near the New Mexico and International Borders received near normal precipitation for May, which is about two to six tenths of an inch depending on elevation, Pinal County received little or no rainfall.  Since the impacts of this drought began with the winter of 2010-2011, most have already been mitigated.  One notable event that occurred on May 9th saw convective outflow initiate substantial blowing dust that propagated northwest across Tucson as well as Pinal County.

The storms produced locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of 1.5 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of both urban flooding and flash flooding. According to a trained spotter located 2 miles southeast of San Tan Mountain, a number of roads in the area were closed at 5 pm due to flash flooding. A Flash Flood Warning was issued for a number of southeast valley communities at 5 pm. Additionally, at 541 pm there was a report by Arizona Department of Transportation about road closures at Riggs Road and Interstate 10, and Riggs at Highway 347 due to flooding. At about 5 pm it was reported that the Hunt Highway was impassable at Gary. No injuries were reported due to the reported flash flooding.

Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to episodes of flash flooding or flooding. Radar and rain gages indicated that rainfall rates exceeded 3 inches per hour at times. A few of the storms produced wet microbursts with damaging wind in excess of 60 mph. Much of the heavy rain and flash flooding affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the southeastern portions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated heavy rain which led to episodes of flash flooding. Beginning around 330 pm, flash flooding developed in the southeastern valley communties, including Gilbert and Chander, and flash flood warnings were issued. Heavy rain then developed further to the north and east, and at 5 pm there was a public report of flash flooding in Goldfield Ranch, on the eastern fringe of the Phoenix metropolitan area. A wash along the Beeline Highway was reported to be flowing rapidly, and flooding out of its banks. Fortunately, no injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix area during the mid afternoon hours on July 31st. One storm produced a wind gust to 64 mph at the Deer Valley airport. The thunderstorms moved slowly north, then congealed and intensified over the community of Anthem. Very heavy rains, with rainfall rates in excess of 3 inches per hour, were observed and they led to episodes of flash flooding in the community of Anthem. One weather observer reported rainfall in excess of 5 inches in his rain gage. Flooding also occurred in the Deadman Wash near Interstate 17 to the west of Anthem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area, including the community of Anthem, during the afternoon hours on July 31st. As the afternoon progressed, the storms congealed and intensified, and they produced extremely heavy rain with peak rainfall rates in excess of 3 inches per hour. One weather observer in Anthem measured over 5 inches of rain within 90 minutes ending about 630 pm. The heavy rain caused the Deadman Wash in Anthem to run heavily, and then flood within the town itself. The floodwaters then moved downstream to the southwest of Anthem, and to the west of Interstate 17. Eventually the floodwaters reached the Carefree Highway and the swiftly flowing water trapped a number of unsuspecting motorists. Reports received from local broadcast media indicated that a number of swift water rescues were needed where Deadman Wash crossed the Carefree Highway. At about 830 pm, 9 persons were rescued including one infant. Helicopters were used in the rescue process. Footage of the rescues was shown on local television channels during the evening news.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding washed out several back country roads in the Grand Canyon-Parashant National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second storm in two days crossed Arizona with heavy snow in the high country. The storms moved across the area at night and over the weekend which make measuring the snowfall from each storm difficult. The following are two day totals in inches: Bellemont 20.5, Flagstaff Airport 20.3, Forest Lakes and 2 miles north of Parks 20, Munds Park 19, Kachina Village/Mountainaire 13-18, North Rim of the Grand Canyon 18, Clints Well 15, 12 miles north of Parks 13, Williams 8.2,  Strawberry 12,   Grand Canyon Village 7, 22 miles east of Payson 18, Tonto Village 11.5, 12 miles NNE of Payson 10, Star Valley 5, Payson 4 inches, mountains west and north of Prescott 6, 25 miles west of Seligman and Seligman 5 inches. A small ski hill sout-southwest of Williams reported 36 inches of new snow out of these two storms. Jacob Lake reprted 6 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagstaff Airport received 11.7 inches of new snow. Munds Park received 7.3 inches, Bellemont 10.8 inches, and Mountainaire 12.5 inches.  Interstate 40 was closed in both directions between Ash Fork and Williams because there were so many drivers having problems driving and staying on the road. The largest collision involved three semi trucks and two passenger vehicles. Interstate 17 south of Flagstaff remained open even though there were numerous slide offs, spin outs, and collisions. Law enforcement officials received hundreds of calls for assistance.
