EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washes overflowed their banks at Diamond M Ranch. 2.25 inches of rain fell in only 40 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stockton Hill Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a total of 16 fires in northwest Arizona and southern Nevada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started 13 fires in northern Mohave County. Several of the fires merged into the 35,000 acre Hobble Complex, which destroyed a few outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 66 was closed from mile marker 1 through mile marker 20 due to sections of the pavement being washed away. Other roads were also flooded in the Oatman area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks and debris were over the road to Willow Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck several power lines in Lake Havasu City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong push of monsoon moisture fueled a five-day outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Numerous storms produced flash flooding and/or severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes in Colorado City had basement flooding, and one road was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching the Pacific Northwest Coast kicked a closed low off the southern California Coast across Arizona.  This closed low brought heavy snow to portions of northern Arizona. The heaviest snow fell along and north of major east-west terrain features such as the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains due to strong orographic lift in the southwest flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow started to fall late afternoon on Thursday December 13 across the Western Mogollon Rim including the Flagstaff area and tapered off Friday afternoon. The storm generally left 3-6 inches of snow with one report of 7 inches. Arizona Department of Public Safety reported about 20 slide offs, two minor collisions, and a jack-knifed semi trailer on Interstate 40.  The Flagstaff Police Department reported 8 traffic accidents. One of those accidents had injuries. The Coconino Country Sheriff's office reported one accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching the Pacific Northwest Coast kicked a closed low off the southern California Coast across Arizona.  This closed low brought heavy snow to portions of northern Arizona. The heaviest snow fell along and north of major east-west terrain features such as the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains due to strong orographic lift in the southwest flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 10 and 16 inches of snow fell above 8000 feet. Four to 5 inches of new snow fell between 6000 and 7000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching the Pacific Northwest Coast kicked a closed low off the southern California Coast across Arizona.  This closed low brought heavy snow to portions of northern Arizona. The heaviest snow fell along and north of major east-west terrain features such as the Mogollon Rim and White Mountains due to strong orographic lift in the southwest flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eighteen to 20 inches of new snow fell in Forest Lakes. Clints Well area received 9 inches and Heber-Overgaard received 8 inches of new snow. Eighteen inches of new snow was reported on Dane Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture crept into southern moisture ahead of a weak upper level trough.  Hail and landspout tornadoes occurred with thunderstorms near the Mexican border.  The cold downrush of wind from these storms generated an outflow boundary that progressed northwest across Pima and Pinal Counties resulting in strong winds and reduced visibility in blowing dust. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter mile or less for several minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dust devil briefly spun up into a landspout tornado just south of Rio Rico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall continued in April with really only day of measurable rainfall in parts of southern Arizona. Therefore, drought conditions continued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted in April across portions of Pinal County. April is normally when the driest three month period across southern Arizona begins. While on the only day of the month with measurable precipitation, April 14th, Pinal County actually received precipitation amounts that were near or slightly above the monthly normal amount for April, this was only enough to keep drought conditions from worsening. The rest of southern Arizona received less than 50 percent of normal monthly rainfall, and in some cases, none at all. While the long term impacts of the drought stretch back to Winter 2010-2011, the impacts continue to be mitigated by being previously dealt with by ranchers, farmers, Forest Service, and other industries and agencies affected. For example, cattle have been sold off and what little herds remain continue to be subsidized by trucking in water and feed. Dry thunderstorms developed on April 23, sparking small wildfires across portions of Cochise and Graham Counties, the largest being the Cedar Fire near Klondyke. Other human caused fires were also reported during the month, including the Apache Pass fire near Ft. Bowie which grew to over 1600 acres before containment was reached.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and rather unstable airmass was in place across southern Arizona on the 4th of July. A weather disturbance moving into southern Arizona from Mexico led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of south central Arizona during the morning hours. A cluster of thunderstorms over western Pinal county led to locally heavy rainfall in the area around Casa Grande; 3 hourly rainfall totals in excess of 1.5 inches were observed. This led to urban flooding, especially in the community of Arizona City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Santa Rosa @ SR 84 Maricopa County Flood Control District gage, 1.54 inches of rainfall were observed a in 3 hour period ending at 11:30 AM on the 4th of July. Similar values were observed at a nearby gage to the east, Santa Cruz @ SR 84. Both gages were located along State Route 84, between the communities of Stanfield and Casa Grande. The heavy rainfall caused the Santa Rosa wash to run heavily. Water ponding on State Route 84 caused the temporary closure of the road during the late morning and early afternoon hours. No accidents were reported on SR 84. The heavy rain was produced by a cluster of thunderstorms that developed ahead of a weather disturbance approaching from northwest Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month of well below normal rainfall resulted in continued extreme drought conditions across portions of south central Arizona in May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued in portions Pinal County throughout May. The overall D3 area remained unchanged from April.  While portions of southeast Arizona near the New Mexico and International Borders received near normal precipitation for May, which is about two to six tenths of an inch depending on elevation, Pinal County received little or no rainfall.  Since the impacts of this drought began with the winter of 2010-2011, most have already been mitigated.  One notable event that occurred on May 9th saw convective outflow initiate substantial blowing dust that propagated northwest across Tucson as well as Pinal County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture crept into southern moisture ahead of a weak upper level trough.  Hail and landspout tornadoes occurred with thunderstorms near the Mexican border.  The cold downrush of wind from these storms generated an outflow boundary that progressed northwest across Pima and Pinal Counties resulting in strong winds and reduced visibility in blowing dust. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter mile or less for several minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several four inch diameter tree limbs were broken in eastern sections of Tucson.  The Tucson ASOS measured a peak gust of 52 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies covered the ground in Rio Rico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet and rather unstable airmass was in place across southern Arizona on the 4th of July. A weather disturbance moving into southern Arizona from Mexico led to scattered showers and thunderstorms across portions of south central Arizona during the morning hours. A cluster of thunderstorms over western Pinal county led to locally heavy rainfall in the area around Casa Grande; 3 hourly rainfall totals in excess of 1.5 inches were observed. This led to urban flooding, especially in the community of Arizona City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers, and isolated thunderstorms, developed across south central Arizona during the morning hours on July 4th, and they produced locally heavy rain which affected the communities of Casa Grande and Maricopa. Based on radar estimates and surrounding rain gage reports, rainfall in excess of one inch fell within a 3 hour period ending by 11:30 am.  At 9:30 am, the Casa Grande Fire Department/Rescue reported local street flooding in Casa Grande with water 3-4 inches deep over area roads. Local media reported road closures from Murphy Road to White and Parker Road along the West Maricopa-Casa Grande Highway southeast of Maricopa, due to the locally heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture crept into southern moisture ahead of a weak upper level trough.  Hail and landspout tornadoes occurred with thunderstorms near the Mexican border.  The cold downrush of wind from these storms generated an outflow boundary that progressed northwest across Pima and Pinal Counties resulting in strong winds and reduced visibility in blowing dust. Visibilities were reduced to a quarter mile or less for several minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies covered the ground in Douglas.  Heavy rain and minor street flooding accompanied the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably strong high pressure and extremely dry conditions resulted in the hottest temperatures so far in 2012 across southern Arizona. These hot conditions caused two tourists to lose their lives while hiking.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 23-year-old female tourist died from heat exhaustion/heat stroke while hiking in the Tortolita Mountains with family. The high temperature at Tucson was 93 degrees. She was pronounced dead at 130 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably strong high pressure and extremely dry conditions resulted in the hottest temperatures so far in 2012 across southern Arizona. These hot conditions caused two tourists to lose their lives while hiking.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 35-year-old female tourist from Germany died rom heat exhaustion/heat stroke at 200 pm while hiking in Saguaro National Park West. The high temperature in Tucson was 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving across northern Arizona brought strong winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds caused blowing dust and sand that reduced the visibility down to 1/2 mile at times along Highway 160 in Cow Springs. The wind speed was 50 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy to very heavy snow fell across the Western Mogollon Rim.  Areas around Flagstaff picked up 18 to 28 inches of snow.  A local ski resort reported an impressive 53 inches of snow above 9000 feet elevation. Northern Arizona University, Coconino Community College, and most other schools cancelled classes in the Flagstaff area.  A 200 mile section of Interstate 40 was closed in both directions between Kingman and Winslow.  A 42 mile section of northbound (up hill) Interstate 17 was closed from the Sedona exit to Flagstaff. The Coconino County Sheriff's search and rescue unit warned people of the avalanche danger in the San Fransisco Peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter two miles north of McNary reported 12.5 inches of snow with this storm. Show Low received 9 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved across Arizona with strong winds ahead of the cold front, heavy rain and snow along and behind the cold front, and then heavy rain and snow showers as the cold pool aloft move across the state with the low center.  Up to four and half feet of snow fell in the Kachina Peaks north of Flagstaff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chino Valley (4570 feet elevation) received 4 inches of snow. Wilhoit received 8 inches of snow at around 4500 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeast Arizona during the middle afternoon hours on June 16th, and they produced gusty outflow winds as high as 45 mph. The winds spread to the northwest, picking up substantial amounts of dust and dirt and generating a dust storm. The dust storm moved through northwest Pinal county, through communities such as Coolidge and Casa Grande, and then into the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeastern Arizona during the middle of the afternoon on June 16th, and they produced gusty outflow winds as high as 45 mph. The winds pushed to the northeast, picking up substantial amounts of dust and dirt and a dust storm was generated. At 435 a trained spotter in Coolidge reported visibility below one quarter mile in blowing dust. At 503 pm, another trained weather spotter in Casa Grande reported a haboob approaching from the southeast. Visibility dropped to less than 1/8 of a mile in dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Rock Fire burned a total of 18,300 acres in June and July.  The Truxton Canyon RAWS was destroyed on June 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Black Rock Fire began on June 27th, and continued into July.  The Truxton Canyon RAWS was destroyed by the fire on June 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Rock Fire burned a total of 18,300 acres in June and July.  The Truxton Canyon RAWS was destroyed on June 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Black Rock Fire continued into July, and was fully contained on July 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on June 27th. The storms produced gusty outflow winds which spread to the northwest and generated dense blowing dust across portions of the central deserts, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Dust storms were reported by a number of trained weather spotters, as visibilities dropped below one quarter of a mile. In addition to the dense blowing dust, damaging microburst winds were also reported in the community of Toltec, in northern Pinal county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over southeastern Arizona during the afternoon hours on June 27th. Gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph spread to the northwest across portions of northern Pinal county and generated dust storm conditions. At 626 pm, a trained spotter in Coolidge reported visibility below 1/4 mile in dense blowing dust. Another spotter in the San Tan Valley, near Combs & Schnepf Farms roads, reported a dust storm at 7 pm. The spotter estimated gusts to 45 mph which lowered visibility to 1/4 mile in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure aloft continued across the southwestern Arizona and southeast California deserts on July 11th, and led to excessive heat. Excessive heat warnings were in effect through 8 pm; despite the warnings the heat turned deadly for a Somerton man who died in his home due to lack of air conditioning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure aloft over the desert southwest led to excessive heat across the southwestern Arizona deserts on July 11th. An excessive heat warning had been in effect since 2 pm Monday, and remained in effect through 8 pm Wednesday. Although high temperatures lowered to around 110 on Wednesday, highs across the western Arizona and southeast California deserts reached between 115 and 118 on the 9th and 10th. Despite the excessive heat warning the heat proved deadly for a Somerton man on July 11th. According to an article in the Yuma Sun, shortly after 8 pm, firefighters were dispatched to a residence in the 3300 block of West Mandarina Street. When the firefighters entered the home, they realized there was no air conditioning and temperatures were in excess of 100 degrees. They found a 72 year old man in his bedroom, unconcious and unresponsive. They removed him from the home and treated him in their ambulance before transferring him to the Yuma Regional Medical Center. Despite the medical care, he passed away. Earlier in the day, at 1130 am, firefighters treated a 37 year old man working outside at the West Cocopah Reservation. The man was experiencing a heat emergency, and was having seizures. He was transported to YRMC and survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over southwest Arizona during the evening hours on the 13th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, some of the storms produced very heavy rainfall. Some of the heaviest rainfall occurred in the Yuma area; the official Yuma weather station measured 1.66 inches of rain which broke the daily rainfall record by approximately one inch. Rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour were observed with the strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across southwestern Yuma county during the evening hours on July 13th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the airmass, some storms were able to generate intense rainfall with rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. At 1015 pm, a trained weather spotter located about one mile west of Yuma International Airport reported a 4 hour rainfall total of 2.4 inches. The same event dropped 1.66 of rain at the official Yuma weather station (Yuma ASOS) and this broke the daily record by almost one inch. The heavy rain led to street flooding and caused area washes to run heavily.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across much of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, many of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to periods of flash flooding. Radar indicated that rainfall totals in excess of 2 inches occurred with some of the stronger storms, and the excessive rain caused washes to flow heavily, water to flow over roads and highways, and significant street flooding. Some communities affected included Bouse and Quartzsite. In addition, isolated thunderstorms generated wet microbursts, which produced gusty and damaging winds in the Quartzsite area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across La Paz county during the early afternoon hours on July 14th. Due to the wet and unstable nature of the airmass, most of the storms produced heavy rain which led to episodes of flash flooding. A few storms managed to produce wet microbursts, leading to gusty and damaging winds. According to APS, an Arizona utility company, gusty winds downed several power poles along Highway 95 north of Quartzsite at about 2 pm. One pole was downed 5 miles to the north, and an additional two were downed 8 miles to the north. Peak wind gusts were estimated to be at least 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High based showers and thunderstorms did not produce rainfall, but gusty winds did occur.  Some of these winds resulted in minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds blew the awning off the front of a mobile home near Ajo and Interstate 19.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed to the south and southwest of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on July 23rd. The storms produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which moved to the north and picked up considerable dust, creating dust storm conditions. The dust storm moved into the greater Phoenix area, primarily affecting the central and western valley communities. The dense blowing dust led to the issuance of a dust storm warning and also created very hazardous driving conditions during the evening rush hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed to the south and southwest of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on July 23rd. The storms produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 30 mph which moved north and picked up copious amounts of dust. A dust storm was generated which moved into the greater Phoenix area and primarily affected the central and western valley communities. At 405 pm, a trained spotter reported visibility below 100 yards in a dust storm at 51st Avenue and Dobbins. At 438 pm a trained spotter in downtown Phoenix at 7th Street and Jefferson reported near zero visibility in dense blowing dust. Additionally, at 445 pm another spotter near Papago Park reported visibility below one quarter mile in blowing dust at 52nd Street and McDowell Road. The reports helped trigger a dust storm warning for the greater Phoenix area. The dense blowing dust created very hazardous driving conditions during the evening rush hour. Fortunately, no significant accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon and evening hours on July 23rd. The atmosphere was rather unstable, and somewhat on the dry side. As a result, the storms that developed were able to generated strong and gusty winds in excess of 58 mph. No damage occurred as a result of the strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon and early evening hours on July 23rd. In addition to producing dense blowing dust in the greater Phoenix area, some of the storms generated strong wind gusts in excess of 50 mph. At 630 pm, a trained spotter near Queen Valley reported a thunderstorm wind gust estimated to be 60 mph. No damage occurred as a result of the strong gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the deserts of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, during the afternoon hours on July 29th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall with rainfall rates in excess of 1.5 inches per hour. The excessive rain led to episodes of flash flooding, primarily in the southeast portions of the Phoenix area, and unfortunately led to the death of a 2 year old boy who was swept down a flooded wash in Queen Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across the southeast portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon hours on July 29th. Due to the very humid and unstable nature of the airmass, the storms were able to produce locally heavy rainfall with rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The rains led to flash flooding; at about 5 pm a Flash Flood Warning was issued for the southeast valley including the community of San Tan Valley. According to the Pinal County Fire Department/Rescue, at 620 pm a 2 year boy died after he was swept down a flooded wash that ran behind his San Tan Valley home. A citizen on an ATV found the child and brought him to emergency crews, who performed CPR on the boy before taking him to Banner Ironwood Hospital. He died later that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Gladiator Fire started on May 13 when a house fire spread into the forest around the community of Crown King. The fire was contained on June 13 at 16,240 acres and cost about 14 million dollars to contain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gladiator Fire began burning on May 13 when a structure fire spread.  A total of six structures were destroyed by the fire.  Eight people suffered injuries. The fire, which covered approximately 16,240 acres, cost $14 million to contain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry weather caused the drought to continue across portions of southeast Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) continued in portions of Pinal County throughout June. The overall D3 area remained unchanged from May. While some areas actually received more rain than normal, those spotty rains amounts only totaled between a tenth and a half of an inch and therefore did little to improve drought conditions, especially since temperatures were above normal.  In fact, the Tucson airport experienced its 5th warmest June on record and had 28 days of 100 degrees or warmer. Since the impacts of this drought began with the winter of 2010-2011, most have already been mitigated. The extent of the dry weather was manifested in dust storm events that occurred on several dates in June.  Thunderstorms that brought the little rain that did fall, also created gusty outflow winds that kicked up dust, ultimately developing into haboobs.  These large areas of blowing dust rolled northward across portions of Pima and Pinal Counties toward Phoenix on three occasions in June.  Two of these dust storms reduced visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile on Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix area during the mid afternoon hours on July 31st. One storm produced a wind gust to 64 mph at the Deer Valley airport. The thunderstorms moved slowly north, then congealed and intensified over the community of Anthem. Very heavy rains, with rainfall rates in excess of 3 inches per hour, were observed and they led to episodes of flash flooding in the community of Anthem. One weather observer reported rainfall in excess of 5 inches in his rain gage. Flooding also occurred in the Deadman Wash near Interstate 17 to the west of Anthem.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed in the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on July 31st. As the afternoon progressed, the storms congealed and intensified. As the storms grew stronger they produced very heavy rain in the Anthem area. The rain led to episodes of flash flooding. At 6 pm a weather observer in the CoCoRaHS network measured 5.02 inches of rain which occurred in the past 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across much of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, many of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to periods of flash flooding. Radar indicated that rainfall totals in excess of 2 inches occurred with some of the stronger storms, and the excessive rain caused washes to flow heavily, water to flow over roads and highways, and significant street flooding. Some communities affected included Bouse and Quartzsite. In addition, isolated thunderstorms generated wet microbursts, which produced gusty and damaging winds in the Quartzsite area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county, starting in the late morning and continuing through the afternoon hours. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, many storms produced heavy rain which led to episodes of flash flooding. Radar indicated that 2 to 3 inches of rain, or more, had fallen in the area around Quartzside and Brenda beginning late in the morning and continuing into the middle of the afternoon. The rain led to the closure of highway 95 at Quartzsite at about 2 pm. At 430 pm, a trained spotter 1 mile west of Quartzside reported that numerous washes in town were running heavily, and many streets were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many locations across northern Arizona experienced a dry winter and a dry spring. This allowed for an active fire season as fuels were already below average at the beginning of the fire season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grand Fire was reported at 1230 PM on May 12th. The fire burned a total of 480 acres in the Grand Canyon National Park and in the Kaibab National Forest. The fire was declared contained by the end of the day on May 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many locations across northern Arizona experienced a dry winter and a dry spring. This allowed for an active fire season as fuels were already below average at the beginning of the fire season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poco Fire was reported at 233 PM on June 14 about six miles northeast of Young, Arizona.  The fire grew to 11, 950 acres before it was declared contained on July 5. Containment cost were estimated to be 9 million dollars. This area experienced several flash floods and debris flows as the rainy season became active in July and August. Fire fire fighters were injured during containment efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across much of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, many of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to periods of flash flooding. Radar indicated that rainfall totals in excess of 2 inches occurred with some of the stronger storms, and the excessive rain caused washes to flow heavily, water to flow over roads and highways, and significant street flooding. Some communities affected included Bouse and Quartzsite. In addition, isolated thunderstorms generated wet microbursts, which produced gusty and damaging winds in the Quartzsite area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county, including the area around Bouse, during the late morning hours and they continued through the afternoon. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced heavy rain which led to episodes of flash flooding. Flash flood warnings were in effect for central La Paz county, including Bouse, beginning at 1238 pm and continued through the afternoon. At 310 pm, a trained weather spotter 1 mile south of Bouse reported that the Bouse wash was running heavily and flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across much of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on July 14th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, many of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to periods of flash flooding. Radar indicated that rainfall totals in excess of 2 inches occurred with some of the stronger storms, and the excessive rain caused washes to flow heavily, water to flow over roads and highways, and significant street flooding. Some communities affected included Bouse and Quartzsite. In addition, isolated thunderstorms generated wet microbursts, which produced gusty and damaging winds in the Quartzsite area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed across much of La Paz county, including the area around Bouse, during the late morning hours on July 14th, and they continued through the afternoon hours. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced heavy rain which led to episodes of flash flooding. Flash flood warning were in effect for central La Paz county, including Bouse, from 1238 pm through the late afternoon. At 5 pm, fire department and rescue personnel reported flash flooding along Highway 72, forcing the closure of the road at mile marker 12 due to water flowing across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 29th. Although many of the storms produced locally heavy rain which led to urban flooding and flash flooding, some storms generated strong, gusty and damaging microburst winds. Most of the wind damage, in communities such as Fountain Hills and San Tan Valley, was due to uprooted trees. Wind speeds were estimated to be in excess of 50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on July 29th and they affected portions of the greater Phoenix area, including the community of Fountain Hills. Some of the thunderstorms produced damaging microburst wind gusts estimated to be at least 65 knots. According to local broadcast media reports, at approximately 230 pm, the strong winds uprooted trees in Fountain Hills and also damaged roof tiles on some homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on July 29th. Although many of the storms produced locally heavy rain which led to urban flooding and flash flooding, some storms generated strong, gusty and damaging microburst winds. Most of the wind damage, in communities such as Fountain Hills and San Tan Valley, was due to uprooted trees. Wind speeds were estimated to be in excess of 50 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on July 29th, and some of the storms generated gusty and damaging microburst winds. According to a trained weather spotter 5 miles northeast of San Tan Valley, at 355 pm gusty winds estimated to be at least 60 knots blew down a number of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low off the coast of Baja California and an upper high over Texas resulted in a weak influx of monsoonal moisture into southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms produced gusty outflow winds and kicked up areas of blowing dust across parts of central and eastern Pima County. The outflows merged in Pinal County creating a haboob along Interstate 10 that progressed northwest toward Phoenix. Visibility was reduced to less than a quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds broke off tree limbs as large as 4 inches in diameter and peeled bark off a tree near Pima Street and Sahuara Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low off the coast of Baja California and an upper high over Texas resulted in a weak influx of monsoonal moisture into southeast Arizona. Thunderstorms produced gusty outflow winds and kicked up areas of blowing dust across parts of central and eastern Pima County. The outflows merged in Pinal County creating a haboob along Interstate 10 that progressed northwest toward Phoenix. Visibility was reduced to less than a quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed tree limbs about a mile west of the Twin Peaks exit of Interstate 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many locations across northern Arizona experienced a dry winter and a dry spring. This allowed for an active fire season as fuels were already below average at the beginning of the fire season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cottonwood Wildfire was reported about 15 miles north of Whiteriver. The fire was about 15 acres at the time that it was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many locations across northern Arizona experienced a dry winter and a dry spring. This allowed for an active fire season as fuels were already below average at the beginning of the fire season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was reported near Wide Ruins at about 10 acres. Fire crews were on-site for at least 5 days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance triggered thunderstorms in weak southwest flow upstream of an upper high which stretched from Kansas to Chihuahua. Thunderstorms produced gusty winds and blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms moved across the Three Points, Picture Rocks and Avra Valley areas, generating strong outflow winds that resulted in a reduced visibility in blowing dust. A haboob was photographed moving through these areas and across south central Pinal County.  Four separate accidents occurred on Interstate 10 between mile markers 214 and 226, but the interstate remained open. One accident near Red Rock resulted in seven injuries when a sport utility vehicle rear ended a cement truck. Two adults in the front seat of the SUV were taken to the hospital with serious although not life-threatening injuries. Another two-vehicle accident in the same area left two people with minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow covered roads and unsafe winter driving lead to at least ten slide offs and collisions in the Flagstaff area on Interstate 40.  At least one vehicle rolled over and one person had to be extracted and was transported to the Flagstaff Medical Center. There were also multiple slide offs and collisions on Interstate 17 near Flagstaff. Both Interstates were blocked for a while. The east bound lanes of I-40 were backed up for as much as 10 miles. State Highway 64 north of Williams was also close for a time due to the weather.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong trough of low pressure brought strong winds, snow, and rain to northern Arizona. Blowing snow and dust impacted travel along Interstate 40 east of Flagstaff to the New Mexico Boarder. Snow covered roads also impacted travel over the high country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ADOT wind sensor at Two Guns recorded sustained winds above 40 MPH and/or wind gusts above 58 MPH for close to three hours. The peak wind gust was 71 MPH.  The peak wind gust in Leupp was 70 MPH. Wupatki reported a peak  wind gust of 59 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced snow at elevations above 3500 feet, especially in Santa Cruz and Cochise counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 8 of snow occurred in the Bisbee area of Cochise County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a thunderstorm fell over the Schultz Fire area caused minor flows into the neighborhood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 0.30 and 0.70 of rain fell in about 15 minutes over the Schultz Fire area and the neighborhood below the fire area. Water was flowing over Brandis Road with rocks up to baseball sized rolling over the road. The maximum flow across the road was about 10 feet wide and a foot deep. Road crews were able to clean the road off. Water was still flowing in the ditches 5 hours later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hail (0.2 to 0.8) covered the ground two miles north of McNary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail (0.2 to 0.8 inches in diameter) covered the ground one inch deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous, cold and wet Pacific upper level low pressure system moved into Arizona during the day on Sunday March 18th. The low brought scattered to numerous rain showers to the central deserts, as well as widepsread rain and snow showers to the higher terrain of southern Gila County. Snow levels fell overnight Sunday night to near 3000 feet, and two to five inches of new snow fell below 5000 feet, affecting communities such as Globe and Miami and forcing the closure of area highways such as US 60. Snow diminished during morning hours on Monday March 19 as the low began to exit to the east of Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong, cold and wet Pacific low pressure system moved into Arizona during the day on Sunday, March 18. The system brought widespread showers of rain and snow to the higher terrain east of Phoenix. Snow levels fell to near 3000 feet overnight Sunday night into Monday morning. A number of trained weather spotters across southern Gila County reported locally heavy snow which began Sunday morning and continued into Monday morning. One weather spotter in Globe at 4600 foot elevation reported a minimum of 5 inches new snow as of 8 AM Monday morning. Several additional reports of 2 inches or more in the Globe-Miami area were received. Conditions at Top of the World were bad enough to close US 60, and conditions were equally as poor on highway 60/77 heading towards Show Low. As of 1030 pm on the 18th, law enforcement and rescue personnel had been dispatched to many accidents. There was a serious accident on highway 77 towards Show Low, but crews were having a difficult time getting through due to snowpacked conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold Pacific storm system brought snow to much of northern Arizona with measurable snow down to 3400 feet elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter two miles north of McNary reported 8.3 inches of snow at an elevation of around 7400 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours on March 18th, a vigorous cold front raced eastward across the central Arizona deserts. The front was associated with a cold, wet Pacific upper level low pressure system. The upper low moved into Arizona during the day on March 18th, and the air mass became unstable. Isolated thunderstorms developed in the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon and evening hours, and a few of them produced strong and gusty winds in excess of 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold and wet Pacific upper low moved into Arizona on March 18th, and the air mass became rather unstable. Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours, and some of them produced strong gusty winds. At 714 pm, the official weather station at Phoenix Sky Harbor airport recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph. No damage was reported at the airport due to the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced snow at elevations above 3500 feet, especially in Santa Cruz and Cochise counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to Eight inches of snow occurred in Southern portions of Eastern Cochise county including the Douglas area westward to Naco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold front brought high winds to much of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin. Isolated heavy snow also fell in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A suspension cable and power lines were snapped on and near the Hoover Dam Bypass bridge; several awnings and roofs were damaged and some sheds blew away in Bullhead City; several trees blew over, one of which hit a power line, and one of which caused a four-car crash that injured two people.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought heavy snow to portions of northeast Arizona. Lighter amounts of snow fell along and north of the Mogollon Rim/Interstate 40 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell in Lukachukai at an elevation of about 6400 feet above mean sea level. Delivery trucks had to turn around because of the snow before they could reach town.  Three inches of snow fell in Dennehotso at an elevation of 5000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought high winds to portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin, along with heavy snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds 4 miles S of Meadview tore the roof off a gazebo and lifted shingles off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought high winds to portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin, along with heavy snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred along Interstate 40 at Fort Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system brought high winds to portions of the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin, along with heavy snow in the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Diamond M Ranch measured a total of 10.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system move from off the southern California coast, across southern Nevada, and then northern Arizona. This brought accumulating snow down to as low at 3500 feet.  The heaviest snow fell over the Eastern Mogollon Rim and White Mountains.  Winter Storm conditions were seen at lower elevations (such as Page) that do not usually see much snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of snow fell in Fredonia at an elevation of 4700 feet. This much snow at this elevation meets the heavy snow criteria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved eastward across southern Arizona on May 9th. Southeast flow ahead of the low advected moisture into eastern Arizona, and scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving east across southern Arizona spread moisture into south central Arizona which led to scattered thunderstorms over the lower deserts during the afternoon hours on May 9th. According to a trained weather spotter, microburst wind gusts estimated at 70 knots damaged 8 mobile homes in the Pleasant Valley Mobile Home Park at approximately 435 PM. The park was located near Germain and Ironwood, about 5 miles east of Queen Creek. Six of the homes had their northeast-facing awnings removed. The strong winds lifted up the overhangs and in some cases removed portions of the roofs. Many of these homes were blown off of their foundations. The strong microburst winds were likely enhanced by a gustnado, which increased the severity of the damage. In addition to the mobile home damage, the strong winds blew down a number of power lines in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved eastward across southern Arizona on May 9th. Southeast flow ahead of the low advected moisture into eastern Arizona, and scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving east across Arizona spread moisture into south central Arizona, and led to the development of scattered afternoon thunderstorms on May 9th. Some of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall, in addition to strong and damaging microburst winds. At 515 PM, a trained weather spotter in east Mesa reported localized street flooding on Broadway road between Sossamon and 80th Street. A nearby Guadalupe Channel ALERT site measured 0.91 inches of rainfall. The street flooding created hazardous driving conditions during the late afternoon rush hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved eastward across southern Arizona on May 9th. Southeast flow ahead of the low advected moisture into eastern Arizona, and scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low moving east across southern Arizona spread moisture into south central Arizona and led to scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours on May 9th. Some of the storms in the Gila Bend area produced damaging microburst winds with peak gusts estimated to be at least 60 knots. As reported by the local utility company, at approximately 615 PM the gusty winds downed a tree which blocked traffic on Pima Road and Highway 85. Construction debris from Interstate 8 and Bypass 85 was strewn about by the strong winds. In addition, power lines were downed by sustained winds reported to be in excess of 45 mph and this left most of the town of Gila Bend without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved eastward across southern Arizona on May 9th. Southeast flow ahead of the low advected moisture into eastern Arizona, and scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms generated strong and damaging microburst winds, in addition to locally heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving eastward across southern Arizona spread moisture into eastern Arizona and scattered thunderstorms developed over the central deserts during the afternoon hours on May 9th. A few of the storms generated strong, damaging microburst winds. At 5 PM, a 58 MPH wind gust was measured four miles north of the town of Maricopa. The wind gust was reported by an official COOP weather observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold and vigorous Pacific low pressure system moved to the northeast and across Arizona on Saturday, May 26th. The associated frontal passage caused high temperatures to fall into the lower 80s over the deserts and also led to occasional strong and gusty winds during the late morning and afternoon hours. One of the stronger wind gusts caused a large fence to topple in Laveen, leading to the death of a 2 year old boy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system and associated cold front moved to the northeast and across the central Arizona deserts during the day on May 26th. The frontal passage produced occasional strong and gusty winds, with peak gusts estimated to be in excess of 35 mph. According to the Arizona Republic, at 1045 in the morning, a wind gust estimated to be around 40 mph caused a 500 pound fence to topple over. The fence, which moved back and forth on a wheel, apparently gave way in a strong wind gust and fell on a 2 year boy, causing his death. The fence struck the young boy in the head.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An low pressure system moved south into southwestern Arizona during the evening hours on May 7th, and triggered some high-based convection in the Yuma area. Despite the lack of thunderstorms in the area - no lightning was observed - virga showers developed and they led to gusty surface winds which were strong enough to knock over 14 power poles. Microburst winds developed despite the seeming lack of any thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved south and into southwest Arizona during the afternoon and evening on May 7th, and some mid level moisture moved in with the upper low. The atmosphere became unstable, and virga showers developed in the Yuma area shortly after midnight. Strong and gusty winds developed in the vicinity of the virga, estimated to be in excess of 50 knots. According to the Yuma Police, at 140 AM on the 8th, 14 power poles were downed at County 14th Street and Avenue 2E in the Tierra Mesa subdivision. In addition, fence and barn roof damage was reported near County 16th Street and Avenue A. There was no indication of thunderstorms in the Yuma area overnight, and no lightning was observed. However, the atmospheric conditions allowed the gusty winds to take on the characteristics of a damaging microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While early winter precipitation allowed for slight improvements in drought conditions across southern Arizona, precipitation waned during the remainder of the winter, causing drought conditions to worsen again in March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November and early December precipitation resulted in widespread improvements in drought conditions across all of southern Arizona. During the first half of December, portions of Pinal County received over 150% of normal rainfall. However, an extremely dry January through March period caused drought conditions to worsen once again. Extreme drought conditions (D3) returned to roughly the northwest half of Pinal County, including far northern sections of the Tohono O'odham Nation by mid March.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN COCONINO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure over the Great Basin swept a strong cold front across Arizona.  Strong to very strong winds developed ahead of the front and produced areas of blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Arizona Department of Transportation wind sensor at Two Guns along Interstate 40 reported wind gust around 58 MPH or higher for around two hours and sustained winds of 30 MPH or higher for about 6 hours.  The peak wind gust was 65 MPH.  Wupatki National Monument had a peak wind gust of 63 MPH.

However, an extremely dry January through March period caused drought conditions to worsen once again. Extreme drought conditions (D3) returned to roughly the northwest half of Pinal County, including far northern sections of the Tohono O'odham Nation by mid March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure resulted in gusty southwest winds across southern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained southwest winds of 25 to 35 miles per hour resulted in minor damage in Sierra Vista. Just after midnight, a wind gust near 50 mph lifted a tent that housed the Sierra Vista Cowboy Church, carrying it over 100 yards, before landing on horse pens. The pens sustained minor damage while the tent received several tears and bent rods, but was able to be repaired. A stop sign was also blown over at the intersection of South 1st St and Witt Drive. A tree was also reported downed on Avenida Tienda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm system brought strong and gusty winds to northern Arizona and locally heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of new snow fell in Jacob Lake overnight.  ADOT reported a total of 12 inches of new snow about a mile north of Jacob Lake by noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon and evening hours on August 20th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated both very heavy rain and strong, gusty damaging winds, estimated to be in excess of 70 mph. Most of the damage occurred in the communities of Mesa and Chandler during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon and early evening hours on August 20th. Some of the thunderstorms generated both very heavy rain and strong, gusty and damaging microburst winds. According to a trained weather spotter in East Mesa, at 715 pm thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to be at least 65 knots blew down a carport and caused damage to the roof of the home to which the carport was attached. In addition, a number of tree branches with diameters up to 3 inches were downed in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging hail and heavy rain northwest of Flagstaff in Doney Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 3/4 inch diameter hail that stripped leaves from trees and ruined a garden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused heavy rain and flash flooding in Chino Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of street flooding in Chino Valley. Some roads were washed out. Some of the impacted roads included Yuma Road, 3 North, Son Shine Land, and Buffalo Run at Highway 89.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain cause water to flow over State Route 97 south of Bagdad.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yavapai County Sheriff Department reported debris along State Route 97 near mile post 163 (about 5 miles south of Bagdad).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz Fire scar caused heavy runoff in the subdivision and streets below.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over the Schultz Fire area.  Heavy run off covered subdivision streets. Runoff covered one lane of Highway 89 with debris and that lane was closed until the water went down and the debris was cleared.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding in and around Tuba City.  Damage in town was minor. Highway 264 was washed out just south of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused minor flooding in much of Tuba City with damaged landscaping and washed out driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding in and around Tuba City.  Damage in town was minor. Highway 264 was washed out just south of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 264 was washed out by a flash flood at mile marker 323 just southeast of Tuba City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex formed over south central Arizona during the early morning hours on August 23rd, a result of a weather disturbance acting on a very humid and unstable airmass. The complex of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across much of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, beginning shortly after midnight and persisting through sunrise. Peak rainfall rates were between 3 and 4 inches per hour, and the heavy rain led to episodes of flooding and flash flooding, affecting areas from Witmann and New River south through the central portions of Phoenix. Several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for the event, but no Flash Flood Warnings were issued, despite the fact that a Flash Flood Watch remained in effect through 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex developed across south central Arizona, including the entire Phoenix metropolitan area, during the early morning hours on August 23rd. The combination of a weak weather disturbance and a very moist and unstable airmass led to widespread thunderstorms with very heavy rain. At 157 AM, a Flood Control District rain gage 3 miles southeast of central Phoenix measured 1.1 inches of rain within a 30 minute period. The gage was located at the Durango Complex. Another rain gage near Sky Harbor Airport recorded 1.14 inches in 30 minutes as well. The heavy rains led to significant urban flooding and prompted the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory for the greater Phoenix area from 145 AM until 345 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex formed over south central Arizona during the early morning hours on August 23rd, a result of a weather disturbance acting on a very humid and unstable airmass. The complex of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across much of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, beginning shortly after midnight and persisting through sunrise. Peak rainfall rates were between 3 and 4 inches per hour, and the heavy rain led to episodes of flooding and flash flooding, affecting areas from Witmann and New River south through the central portions of Phoenix. Several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for the event, but no Flash Flood Warnings were issued, despite the fact that a Flash Flood Watch remained in effect through 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, including the community of Wittman, during the very early morning hours on August 23rd. The combination of a weather disturbance and a very humid and unstable airmass allowed the thunderstorms to produce locally heavy rainfall, with peak rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The first rains began shortly after midnight, and continued into the early morning hours. At approximately 350 AM, local Fire Department and Rescue units reported a water rescue at 211th Avenu and West Montgomery Road, one mile southeast of the town of Wittman. A vehicle was stuck in rapidly rising water, and the occupants needed to be rescued. Although no Flash Flood Warning were in effect at the time, an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was in effect and continued through 4 AM. No injures were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the northern portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the evening hours on August 16th. The storms produced locally heavy rainfall with rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, which affected the towns of Surprise and Circle City. The flooding led to water across area roads, which promted a number of swift water rescues in the west valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across northern portions of Maricopa county during the evening hours, and the storms generated locally heavy rainfall with rain rates in excess of one inch per hour. The heavy rain led to excessive urban flooding, which prompted the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory at 9 pm for north central portions of Maricopa county, including the town of Circle City. According to local law enforcement, at about 945 pm water was reported to be rapidly running across the road at the intersection of 211th Avenue and Highway 74. This intersection is located 4 miles east of Circle City. Law enforcement also indicated that water was flowing over the road at 170th Avenue and Dixileta Road, about 6 miles northwest of Beardsley. This flooding occurred at about 950 pm. No injuries were reported as a result of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the afternoon and evening hours on August 20th. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated both very heavy rain and strong, gusty damaging winds, estimated to be in excess of 70 mph. Most of the damage occurred in the communities of Mesa and Chandler during the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix area during the late afternoon and early evening hours on August 20th, and they affected east valley communities such as Mesa. Due to the very humid and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the thunderstorms were able to produce very heavy rain with rain rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. An automated Flood Control District gage located near the intersection of Higley and Broadway road measured 1.26 inches of rain between 716 and 801 pm. In addition, a trained weather spotter located about 6 miles east of Mesa measured 1.25 inches of rain between 700 and 735 pm. The intense rainfall led to urban flooding across the eastern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex formed over south central Arizona during the early morning hours on August 23rd, a result of a weather disturbance acting on a very humid and unstable airmass. The complex of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across much of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, beginning shortly after midnight and persisting through sunrise. Peak rainfall rates were between 3 and 4 inches per hour, and the heavy rain led to episodes of flooding and flash flooding, affecting areas from Witmann and New River south through the central portions of Phoenix. Several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for the event, but no Flash Flood Warnings were issued, despite the fact that a Flash Flood Watch remained in effect through 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convective complex developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the early morning hours on August 23rd. The combination of a passing weather disturbance and a very humid and unstable airmass allowed the thunderstorms to produce copious rainfall with peak rain rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. Heavy rain began in central Phoenix around 130 AM and continued into the early morning hours. According to broadcast media reports, a van became stuck in a flooded wash at 7700 East Roosevelt Street at about 452 AM. The wash was located about 3 miles east of Papago Park. As a result, the Scottsdale Fire and Rescue Unit performed a water rescue to save the occupants of the van from the flood waters. No Flash Flood Warnings were in effect at the time, however a Flash Flood Watch was in effect through 5 AM, and Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories had been issued earlier for the area. Fortunately, no injuries were reported as a result of the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county on during the afternoon hours on August 29th, and a few of them generated strong, gusty and damaging outflow winds. The winds, estimated to be in excess of 50 knots, blew down a number of power poles on U.S. Highway 95 in the northwest part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county during the afternoon hours on August 29th. The stronger storms were able to generate strong and gusty microburst winds. According to local law enforcement, at 430 pm gusty outflow winds estimated to be in excess of 50 knots blew down several power poles between mileposts 127 and 129 on U.S. Highway 95, approximately 13 miles southeast of Poston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the late morning hours across portions of La Paz county, and affected communities such as Bouse and Brenda. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to isolated episodes of flash flooding. Heavy rain resulted in water flowing across highway 72 just to the northwest of Bouse. No accidents or injuries were reported as a result of the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed around Bouse during the late morning hours, and they produced locally heavy rainfall; radar indicated that peak rain rates were in excess of one inch per hour.  At 1205 pm, the Arizona Department of Transportation reported water flowing across Highway 72 approximately 2 miles northwest of Bouse. A Flash Flood Warning was not in effect, however a Small Stream Flood Advisory had been issued for Bouse at 1148 am. No accidents or injuries were reported as a result of the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex formed over south central Arizona during the early morning hours on August 23rd, a result of a weather disturbance acting on a very humid and unstable airmass. The complex of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across much of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, beginning shortly after midnight and persisting through sunrise. Peak rainfall rates were between 3 and 4 inches per hour, and the heavy rain led to episodes of flooding and flash flooding, affecting areas from Witmann and New River south through the central portions of Phoenix. Several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for the event, but no Flash Flood Warnings were issued, despite the fact that a Flash Flood Watch remained in effect through 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex developed across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the early morning hours on August 23rd. The combination of a passing weather disturbance and a very humid airmass allowed thunderstorms to produce excessive rainfall with peak rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. Heavy rains began in the central Phoenix area by 130 AM, and persisted into the early morning hours. According to the local Department of Highways, mud and debris were blocking the right 3 lanes of eastbound Interstate 10 at about 515 AM. The debris flow was a result of flash flooding. The blockage was located 2 miles east of Tolleson. Although Flash Flood Warnings were not issued, a Flash Flood Watch was in effect through 5 AM. Earlier, Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for the central Phoenix area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on August 20th, and they affected communities such as Quartzsite, Salome and Harcuvar. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated very heavy rains with rainfall rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, which caused area washes to flow heavily and overflow their banks, and many roads to become flooded as well. Fortunately, no injuries were reported as a consequence of the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on August 20th, and they affected the area around Quartzsite. Due to the very humid and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the thunderstorms generated very heavy rainfall with rainfall rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, mostly across the northern portions of Quartzsite. Many of the flash flooding impacts developed around 630 pm and continued into the evening hours. According to the Quartzsite Police Department, at 630 pm the Tyson wash 5 miles north of Quartzsite was running heavily and covering state route 95. Both the Quartzsite Police Department and Arizona Department of Transportation responded and they set up road closures and restrictions along the highway. The heavy rains led to other road closures around the community of Quartzsite; other streets that were impacted included Riggles Avenue, Quail Trail Street and Plymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona during the evening hours on September 2nd, and they generated strong and gusty outflow winds. The winds stirred up significant amounts of dust and dirt over northwest Pinal county; this led to dense blowing dust and dust storm conditions. Visibility lowered to below one quarter mile at times in locations such as Arizona City, and led to the issuance of a Dust Storm Warning for the central deserts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the early evening hours across south central Arizona, and they generated gusty outflow winds in excess of 35 mph which spread westward across northwest Pinal county. The gusty winds stirred up significant amounts of dust and dirt and generated dust storm conditions. A dust storm warning was issued for the central deserts from 653 pm and continued until nearly 9 pm. At  638 pm, a trained weather spotter located in Arizona City reported visibility down to one eighth of a mile in dense blowing dust. The sharply reduced visibility created very hazardous driving conditions; fortunately, no accidents or injuries were reported due to the dense blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex formed over south central Arizona during the early morning hours on August 23rd, a result of a weather disturbance acting on a very humid and unstable airmass. The complex of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain across much of south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, beginning shortly after midnight and persisting through sunrise. Peak rainfall rates were between 3 and 4 inches per hour, and the heavy rain led to episodes of flooding and flash flooding, affecting areas from Witmann and New River south through the central portions of Phoenix. Several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories were issued for the event, but no Flash Flood Warnings were issued, despite the fact that a Flash Flood Watch remained in effect through 5 am.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large convective complex developed across south central Arizona, including the greater Phoenix area, during the early morning hours on August 23rd. The combination of a passing weather disturbance and a very humid airmass allowed thunderstorms to produce excessive rain, with peak rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rains initially developed in the Wittmann area after 130 AM, but they persisted off and on through sunrise. According to a trained weather spotter 2 miles southwest of Wittmann, at about 6 AM flooding was reported along Patton Road. Water up to 6 inches deep was reported to be flowing across the road for at least a one mile stretch of the roadway. Flash Flood Warnings were not in effect at the time, but an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory had been issued and was in effect through 9 AM. No injuries were reported in association with the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture began to return from the southeast, fueling strong thunderstorms over the eastern Mojave Desert. Several storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew trampolines into power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of La Paz county during the afternoon and evening hours on August 20th, and they affected communities such as Quartzsite, Salome and Harcuvar. Due to the very moist and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms generated very heavy rains with rainfall rates well in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, which caused area washes to flow heavily and overflow their banks, and many roads to become flooded as well. Fortunately, no injuries were reported as a consequence of the flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across central La Paz county during the afternoon hours on August 20th. Due to the very humid and unstable nature of the atmosphere, the storms produced very heavy rainfall with rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to episodes of flash flooding, which affected the communities of Salome and Harcuvar. The flooding led to the issuance of a Flash Flood Warning at 250 pm, and the warning extended until 445 pm. According to local law enforcement, flooding and flash flooding was observed in the washes around both Salome and Harcuvar. No injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across far northeastern Maricopa county during the early evening hours on September 3rd, and they produced locally heavy rainall that affected the Sunflower Burn area, including Sycamore creek and the town of Sunflower. An automated rain gage in the upper portion of the burn reported heavy rain in a two hour period which resulted in enhanced flow in Sycamore Creek. However, this did not appear to result in significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with locally heavy rain developed over portions of far northeast Maricopa county during the late afternoon hours on September 3rd, and they affected the Sunflower burn area. Locally heavy rain over the burn resulted in enhanced flow down Sycamore creek, as shown by streamflow gages. At 647 pm, the Upper Sunflower RAWS automated rain gauge measured 1.65 inches of rainfall over the past 2 hour period. The heavy rain did lead to enhanced flow down Sycamore creek and as a result a Flash Flood Warning was issued for both the burn area and Sycamore creek downstream through Sunflower. However, it appears that no significant flash flooding actually occurred in the burn area or downstream in the community of Sunflower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very moist air with around 1.75 inches of precipitable water contributed to the development of thunderstorms across much of southeast Arizona. Some storms produced very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding forced the closure of Highway 85 between Why and Organ Pipe National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest air flow in the lower levels resulted in some drying at the surface while mid level moisture remained. Thunderstorm activity was scattered across southeast Arizona anywhere north and east of Sells. The drying at the low levels increased the potential for damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst caused extensive damage to a mobile home park east of Interstate 10 and south of Cortaro Farms Road. An awning was blown over one home, causing part of the roof to peel off, which fell on a vehicle. Other homes suffered skirting, awning and minor roof damage and one tree were uprooted. Another tree was uprooted farther north near Bald Eagle Road and Condor Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the northern portions including communties such as Scottsdale and Carefree. Some of the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain which led to urban as well as flash flooding. Gusty and damaging microburst winds were also associated with some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area, including the community of Scottsdale, during the afternoon hours on August 24th. The stronger storms generated both locally heavy rain and gusty, damaging microburst winds. According to a trained weather spotter located 4 miles northwest of Scottsdale, gusty winds estimated to be at least 60 knots blew down a number of trees with diameters of at least 8 inches. Heavy rain that accompanied the storms lowered visibility to near zero and led to some urban street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area, especially the northern portions including communties such as Scottsdale and Carefree. Some of the thunderstorms produced locally heavy rain which led to urban as well as flash flooding. Gusty and damaging microburst winds were also associated with some of the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the afternoon hours on August 24th and they affected the community of Scottsdale. The stronger storms generated locally heavy rain with peak rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. According to a trained weather spotter located 3 miles northwest of Scottsdale, 0.70 inches of rain fell between 4 pm and 430 pm. The heavy rain led to minor street flooding with water 4 to 6 inches deep across the street. An Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory was in effect through 630 pm for the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the far southwest portions of Maricopa county during the evening hours on August 30th. Some of the storms produced locally heavy rainfall which led to flash flooding south of Gila Bend and along Highway 85. The excessive rain caused area washes to run full and caused water to flow across Highway 85. A Flash Flood Warning was issued as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the far southwest portion of Maricopa county during the evening hours on August 30th. Some of the storms generated locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. Some of the heaviest rain fell south of Gila Bend, and affected traffic along Highway 85. According to local law enforcement, at 717 pm water was running across U.S. Highway 85 and washes were running full at milepost 22. Fortunately, no injuries or water rescues were reported as a result of the heavy rain and flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture began to return from the southeast, fueling strong thunderstorms over the eastern Mojave Desert. Several storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three power poles were blown down in Dolan Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture began to return from the southeast, fueling strong thunderstorms over the eastern Mojave Desert. Several storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pierce Ferry Road was closed at mile marker 15 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture began to return from the southeast, fueling strong thunderstorms over the eastern Mojave Desert. Several storms produced severe weather and/or flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud washed onto Diamond Creek Road north of Peach Springs.
